cross_connections.display_label: Edwards won his seat — then handed it back
Talcott cousins who both supplied Washington's army from CT
EH notes their Standley cousinship — before he married a Hooker
Raised him from age four after his father died
His grandmother Elizabeth Standley was sister to Abigail Standley Hooker.

notable.notable_blurb: Commissary General; Rochambeau's host; 2nd President Bank NY

narrative_blocks.header: Commissary General to both Washington and Rochambeau
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Commissary General of the Continental Army from April 1778, then assumed the same role for Rochambeau's entire French force when it arrived in July 1780. By 1781 he had marshaled 855 horses, 600 oxen, 239 wagon conductors, and bread ovens along the route from Newport to the Hudson — making the Yorktown campaign possible.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington and Rochambeau strategized in his Hartford parlor
narrative_blocks.body: On September 20-22, 1780, Washington and Rochambeau held their war strategy conference at his Hartford home, joined by Hamilton, Knox, and Lafayette. The conference ended abruptly when news of Benedict Arnold's treason at West Point reached them.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised by the uncle who became his brother-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Matthew Talcott raised him from age four after his father died — and shipped him before the mast to improve his health. Years later he married Mehitable Russell, sister of Matthew Talcott's wife Mary, making his guardian also his brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Second president of the Bank of New York
narrative_blocks.body: He served as second President of the Bank of New York 1785-1786, working closely with his friend Alexander Hamilton on the early Republic's financial architecture. Edward Hooker called him 'one of the few men to whom the Revolutionary War proved a source of wealth.'

narrative_blocks.header: Father of the man who founded Wadsworth Atheneum
narrative_blocks.body: His son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — the oldest continuously operating public art museum in America. The museum opened in 1844, four decades after Jeremiah's death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards beat him then handed back the seat
narrative_blocks.body: In 1790 Pierpont Edwards — fellow Hartford figure and Hooker descendant — defeated him for the Connecticut Congressional seat. Edwards then declined to take it, forcing a special election that returned Wadsworth to Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII Liberty ship named in his honor
narrative_blocks.body: During World War II the United States Liberty ship SS Jeremiah Wadsworth was named in his honor. It was a fitting echo of his Revolutionary commissary work — Liberty ships served as workhorses of America's wartime supply effort, just as he had supplied Washington and Rochambeau.

institutions.institution_blurb: His 1784 portrait by John Trumbull (ART030) hangs in the Atheneum his son Daniel founded.
Part of Hartford's elite network that financed the Connecticut Land Company.

education.institution_blurb: Honorary degrees from Yale and Dartmouth, 1796.

Part of Hartford's elite network that financed the Connecticut Land Company.

education.institution_blurb: Honorary degrees from Yale and Dartmouth, 1796.

cross_connections.display_label: He raised her future husband — and was already married to her sister

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage united Hartford's two founding bloodlines
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Russell descended from Thomas Hooker through her mother Mary Pierpont Russell, granddaughter of Rev. James Pierpont and Mary Hooker. When she married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth in 1767 — himself a Talcott descendant through his mother Abigail Talcott — their children became the convergence of the two men who had founded Hartford together in 1636: Thomas Hooker and John Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: The man who raised her husband had married
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable's sister Mary Russell had married Matthew Talcott of Middletown in 1740. When Rev. Daniel Wadsworth died in 1747 — leaving four-year-old Jeremiah fatherless — Matthew took the boy in and raised him. So when Mehitable married Jeremiah in 1764, she married into a household she already knew: the home of her own brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Jeremiah Wadsworth died on April 30, 1804, at age 60. Mehitable lived until March 29, 1817 — another thirteen years, to age 82. She outlived her daughter Harriet, who had died in Bermuda at 23.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousins twice over before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband descended from Elizabeth Standley, whose sister Abigail had married John Hooker of Farmington. The Standley sisters meant the Wadsworth and Hooker lines were already kin — her marriage doubled a connection a century old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Mother of Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330), who founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of the Revolution’s Commissary General

narrative_blocks.header: Died in Bermuda at 23
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Wadsworth was 23 when she died in Saint George's, Bermuda in April 1793, buried at Saint Peter's Church. She was the first child of Jeremiah and Mehitable — the first living embodiment of the Hooker-Talcott convergence — and she died three years before her brother Daniel was old enough to begin building what would become the Wadsworth Atheneum. Her twin sister (unnamed) had died at birth.

cross_connections.display_label: Father-in-law: governor and 2nd US House Speaker
painted the Charter Oak now in this Atheneum

notable.notable_blurb: Founded America's oldest public art museum, 1844

institutions.institution_blurb: Founded the Wadsworth Atheneum 1842-44 — oldest continuously operating public art museum in the United States.
Yale connection through family; major Hartford cultural patron.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded America's oldest continuously operating art museum
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford in 1842, opening it in 1844. A patron of the Hudson River School, he supported Thomas Cole and Frederic Church and built the museum to make their work permanently accessible to Hartford. His father's 1784 portrait by John Trumbull — painted when Daniel was 13 — now hangs in the institution he created, a portrait becoming its own home.

narrative_blocks.header: Both founding bloodlines, Hooker and Talcott, converged
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel's mother Mehitable Russell descended from Mary Hooker Pierpont, daughter of Thomas Hooker. His father Jeremiah descended from Abigail Talcott, granddaughter of Lt.-Col. John Talcott — who crossed the Atlantic in the same ship as Thomas Hooker in 1636. The two founders of Hartford, separated by 130 years and multiple generations, converged in Daniel Wadsworth — and in the museum bearing his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Faith Trumbull, governor's daughter, no children
narrative_blocks.body: He married Faith Trumbull (1769-1846), daughter of Jonathan Trumbull Jr. — Connecticut Governor and 2nd Speaker of the US House of Representatives. The couple had no children, leaving the Atheneum as his primary legacy.

narrative_blocks.header: Discovered Frederic Church, launched his career
narrative_blocks.body: He recognized Hartford-born Frederic Edwin Church's prodigious talent and wrote a letter of introduction vouching for the young man's natural ability — securing him a place to live and study with Thomas Cole. Church became the most famous and financially successful artist in America.

parents.father_research_notes: Jonathan Trumbull Jr. — CT Governor and 2nd Speaker of US House

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of an author and a Union general
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Wadsworth married Nathaniel Terry, one of Hartford's most prominent citizens, in 1798. The Hooker-Talcott blood that ran through her passed to her grandchildren: Rose Terry Cooke became one of New England's most celebrated regional authors, and Gen. Alfred Howe Terry led the Union assault on Fort Wagner and later commanded the Department of the Dakotas — present at the aftermath of the Little Bighorn. Catherine never lived to see either of them distinguish themselves; she died in 1841.

notable.notable_blurb: 4th Mayor Hartford; US Congressman; bank president

burial.plot_notes: d. New Haven CT. Burial location unverified — possibly Grove Street Cemetery New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Hartford for seven turbulent years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the 4th Mayor of Hartford from 1824 to 1831, spanning the city's transition from Federalist to Whig politics. He had previously served one term in the US House of Representatives (1817-1819) as a Federalist, and was a delegate to the 1818 Connecticut Constitutional Convention.

narrative_blocks.header: President of Hartford Fire Insurance for 25 years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as president of the Hartford Fire Insurance Company from 1810 to 1835 — a 25-year tenure spanning the company's early growth into one of New England's leading insurers. He simultaneously served as president of the Hartford Bank from 1819 to 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale-educated landscape gardener of Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale College, Henry returned to Hartford and worked as a landscape gardener — a refined avocation more than a commercial trade in the 1820s. He is buried at Collinsville Cemetery, named for the village where his daughter Alice married Howard Collins.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale College 1820; Yale Medical degree 1823
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1820 — the second son of General Nathaniel Terry (Yale 1786) and Catherine Wadsworth. He continued at the Yale Medical School and received his MD in spring 1823.

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly at 43 in his native city
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine in Hartford for two decades after settling there following Yale. He died very suddenly on December 22, 1843, in his 44th year. His widow Sophia survived him by 25 years.

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law before turning to military career as Union general.

education.notes: Graduated 1833 New York City. Medical degree.

career.notes: Held the chair of obstetrics until his death.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother's sister married into the Hooker family

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle hid Connecticut's Charter in the Oak
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Daniel Wadsworth's uncle Joseph Wadsworth was the man who seized the Connecticut Charter on the night of October 31, 1687 and hid it in the Charter Oak in Hartford — aided by Lt.-Col. John Talcott, the grandfather of Daniel's future wife Abigail Talcott. Daniel himself had no part in it; he was born 17 years later. But both families whose founding myth was the Charter Oak — Wadsworth and Talcott — converged in his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Wadsworth died on November 12, 1747, when his son Jeremiah was four years old. The boy was sent to be raised by his Talcott uncle Matthew Talcott of Middletown — his mother Abigail's brother. The man who would become Commissary General of the Continental Army and intimate friend of Washington grew up in a Talcott household, knowing his father's family only by reputation.

education.institution_blurb: Yale College 1726. Member of Yale Corporation 1743 until his death in 1747.

narrative_blocks.header: Her four daughters never married; only the son
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Talcott, daughter of Connecticut governor Joseph Talcott, married the Reverend Daniel Wadsworth, Hartford's pastor and a member of the Yale Corporation. Of their six children, the four daughters who survived infancy all died unmarried; only her son Jeremiah, who wed Mehitable Russell, carried the family forward.

sources.short: Yale Biographical Sketches (X00418 Rev. Daniel Wadsworth entry)

cross_connections.display_label: Elizabeth Standley and her sister Abigail Standley each married into.
Elizabeth Standley and her sister Abigail Standley each married into.
Her sister Abigail Standley married Hon. John Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Died seven days after birthing her last child
narrative_blocks.body: She gave birth to her seventh child Mary on 4 September 1713 and died seven days later on 11 September — almost certainly of childbirth complications. The infant Mary survived; her widowed father Deacon John Wadsworth raised her with the six older children.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband then married his cousin Samuel's widow
narrative_blocks.body: After her death John Wadsworth married Mary (Humphries) Gridley — who had previously been the second wife of his cousin Samuel Wadsworth. Hartford's tightly-bound Wadsworth households thus shared widows across the family lines.

sources.short: Yale Biographical Sketches; Edward Hooker 1909 entry 362

narrative_blocks.header: Married cousin Samuel's widow as second wife
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Elizabeth Standley died in September 1713 of childbirth complications, he took Mrs Mary (Humphries) Gridley to wife — previously the second wife of his cousin Samuel Wadsworth. The two cousins thus shared a widow within the close-knit Farmington-Hartford Wadsworth family.

political_offices.title: Governor
political_offices.notes: First Governor of Connecticut born within its limits. Longest-serving after Gov. Winthrop. Died in office Oct 11, 1741.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law signed his estate — 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker'
His granddaughter Esther married Rev. Noadiah Russell in 1758
was the Deputy Governor whose death opened his path

notable.notable_blurb: First Connecticut governor born within its limits

narrative_blocks.header: First Connecticut governor born within the colony
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hartford on November 16, 1669, Joseph Talcott was the first Governor of Connecticut born within the colony rather than in England. He served from 1724 until his death on October 11, 1741 — 17 years in office, longer than any predecessor except Governor Winthrop and any successor until the modern era. His funeral expenses of £69 12s. 6d. were paid from the Colonial Treasury.

narrative_blocks.header: He presided in the upper house the afternoon
narrative_blocks.body: When his wife Eunice died suddenly about noon on Thursday, May 25, 1738, the Deputy Governor was absent at Norwich. Governor Talcott did not let his private grief interfere with his official duty — he returned and presided in the upper house that same afternoon. The General Assembly appointed a committee to draw an address of condolence to the Governor.

narrative_blocks.header: His estate was signed by 'Nathaniel Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: At the Court of Probate, Hartford, on December 25, 1742, the distribution of Governor Talcott's estate was signed by all children as co-heirs. Among the signatures: 'Nathaniel Hooker and Eunice Hooker' — his daughter Eunice Talcott and her husband Nathaniel Hooker 2d. The Talcott-Hooker convergence was entered into the public record.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his children each found a separate
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Eunice married Nathaniel Hooker 2d — direct Hooker surname. His son Matthew married Mary Russell, a Hooker descendant through the Pierpont branch. His daughter Abigail married Rev. Daniel Wadsworth; their son Jeremiah later married Mary's own sister Mehitable Russell, also a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Petitioned for his father's estate by primogeniture
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Talcott was the fourth son of Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. but the eldest to survive his father. In 1691, at age 21, he petitioned the General Court for his father's estate by right of primogeniture — an assertive legal claim for colonial Connecticut. He was first mentioned in public life in 1711 as a committee member to lay out the town of Coventry.

bio.notes: Widow of [first husband Wakeman] before marrying Gov. Joseph Talcott (T00045)

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

INST052.name: Wadsworth Atheneum Museum of Art
INST052.notes: Founded 1842 by Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330); opened 1844. Oldest continuously operating public art museum in the United States. The building was designed to house Daniel's personal art collection and establish Hartford as a cultural center. Daniel was a patron of the Hudson River School — Thomas Cole and Frederic Church both exhibited here. The museum holds ART030: John Trumbull's 1784 portrait of Daniel (age 13) and his father Jeremiah (T00387) — painted 58 years before the museum existed, now hanging in the institution Daniel built. A portrait becomes its own home. Daniel Wadsworth descends from BOTH Thomas Hooker (through his mother H00362 Mehitable Russell → H00118 Mary Pierpont → H00034 Mary Hooker Pierpont) AND from John Talcott (through his father T00387 Jeremiah → T00068 Abigail Talcott → T00045 Gov. Joseph Talcott → T00035 Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr.). Two founding Hartford lines united in one institution.

INST0045.name: Connecticut Land Company
INST0045.notes: Formed 1795 by 36 Connecticut investors who purchased the entire Connecticut Western Reserve from the State of Connecticut for over $1,000,000 — land the state had 'reserved' for citizens whose property was damaged by the British in the Revolution. In April 1807, Richfield Township was acquired for $26,087 by seven men: Col. Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757), Solomon Lathrop, Jeremiah Wilcox, Uriel Holmes Jr., Pierpont Edwards (H00386), Daniel Green, and Jonathan Dwight Sr. — two of the seven being Hooker descendants. Pierpont Edwards (H00386) invested $60,000 — approximately 5% of total company holdings. His son John Stark Edwards (HD2044) served as his first agent in the Reserve, but died Feb 22, 1813 in Warren, Trumbull County OH age 35 — the year Whittlesey rode through the black swamp and nearly died at Fort Meigs. Pierpont Township, Ashtabula County, Ohio is named for Edwards. In 1811, Heman Oviatt (husband of HD2934 Eunice Newton Oviatt) bought the northwest quarter of Richfield Township from Col. Tallmadge for $1.25 an acre. John Newton (HD2925) arrived in Richfield in the early 1820s and purchased most of the east center land, building the home later owned by Homer Carter. Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) arrived in Canfield June 27, 1806 — the same summer the company was active — and became public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve. The company was ultimately a financial failure. (Richfield Township History; Ohio History Journal; Whittlesey Genealogy #310)

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST035.name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
INST035.notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.
